title: The Wealth Gap: How the Richest 1% Came to Own More Than the Bottom 50% Combined

sections.title: The Number That Changes Everything
sections.tts_script: Ten people. Just ten. Ten people own more wealth than the bottom three point one billion humans combined. That's not a typo. Ten versus three billion, one hundred million. If you stacked one-dollar bills to represent the net worth of Elon Musk alone, that stack would reach the moon. And back. Four times. This video is not about jealousy. It's about math. About how the rules of money were written — and who wrote them.
sections.visual_notes: Dramatic counter: 10 vs 3,100,000,000. Then Musk wealth visualization. Dark cinematic tone.

sections.title: The Numbers Are Real
sections.tts_script: Let's start with the facts. According to the Credit Suisse Global Wealth Report, the richest one percent of humanity owns forty-five percent of all private wealth on Earth. Every house. Every company. Every stock portfolio. Every yacht. The bottom fifty percent — three point nine billion people — share just two point three percent. Think about that for a second. Half the human species fighting over two percent of the pie. The top one percent holds the equivalent of two hundred and one trillion dollars. The bottom half together hold ten trillion. That's a twenty-to-one ratio. And it's getting worse every single year.
sections.visual_notes: Pie chart animation. Bar charts showing 45% vs 2.3%. World map with wealth distribution heat map.

sections.title: COVID Made It Worse — Fast
sections.tts_script: In March 2020, the world locked down. Millions lost their jobs. Restaurants closed. Airlines collapsed. Entire industries disappeared overnight. And the ten richest men on Earth doubled their wealth. From seven hundred billion to one point five trillion dollars — in less than two years. A new billionaire was created every thirty hours during the pandemic. Every thirty hours. Meanwhile, one million people fell into extreme poverty every thirty-three hours. The pandemic didn't create inequality. It accelerated it. Like rocket fuel poured onto a fire that was already burning.
sections.visual_notes: Split screen: empty streets vs stock market going up. Animated timeline 2020-2022. Counter of new billionaires.

sections.title: The Math That Explains Everything — r > g
sections.tts_script: French economist Thomas Piketty spent twenty years studying wealth data going back to the eighteenth century. His conclusion was simple, and devastating. When the return on capital is greater than economic growth — when your money makes money faster than workers create new wealth — inequality compounds like interest. Exponentially. Historically, capital returns about five percent per year. Economic growth averages around two percent. That three percent gap doesn't sound like much. But over decades, it means the people who own things — stocks, property, businesses — get richer automatically. While the people who work — who only have their labor to sell — fall further behind. It's not about working harder. It's about which rules you're playing by.
sections.visual_notes: Animated formula: r > g. Graph showing diverging lines over time. Historical wealth data visualization.

sections.title: The Tax System That Was Designed to Fail
sections.tts_script: In 1960, the top income tax rate in the United States was ninety-one percent. Ninety-one. That means if you earned a million dollars, you paid nine hundred ten thousand in taxes. And yet, this was the era of America's greatest economic boom. The middle class exploded. Home ownership hit record highs. Universities were almost free. Then, over the following sixty years, that rate was cut. And cut. And cut again. Today it's thirty-seven percent. And that's just the stated rate. In practice, America's billionaires pay around eight percent. Less than a schoolteacher. Less than a nurse. How? Through legal mechanisms: capital gains taxed at lower rates than income, offshore accounts, charitable foundations, carried interest loopholes. Amazon — one of the most profitable companies in history — paid zero dollars in federal income taxes in 2017. Zero. And it was completely legal.
sections.visual_notes: Timeline showing US tax rate drop from 91% to 37%. Amazon $0 tax graphic. Comparison: billionaire 8% vs teacher 22%.

sections.title: CEO Pay vs. The Worker
sections.tts_script: In 1978, the average American CEO earned thirty times more than the typical worker. Thirty times. That ratio felt large. But by 2023, according to the Economic Policy Institute, the ratio had grown to three hundred forty-four to one. Three hundred forty-four. If a worker earns fifty thousand dollars a year, their CEO earns seventeen million. The worker's salary grew with inflation — slowly. The CEO's compensation exploded: stock options, performance bonuses, golden parachutes. The work at the top didn't become three hundred times harder. The rules changed.
sections.visual_notes: Animated comparison: 30:1 ratio growing to 344:1. Bar chart. Worker vs CEO silhouettes with growing gap.

sections.title: Born Rich. Stay Rich.
sections.tts_script: There's another factor no one likes to talk about. Inheritance. Research from the Paris School of Economics shows that inherited wealth accounts for fifty to sixty percent of all wealth accumulation in Western societies. The American Dream says anyone can make it if they work hard enough. But data tells a different story. If you were born in the bottom twenty percent of the income distribution, you have less than a ten percent chance of reaching the top twenty percent in your lifetime. If you were born in the top twenty percent, you have a sixty percent chance of staying there. The game isn't rigged. The starting positions are.
sections.visual_notes: Social mobility chart. Inheritance wealth flow visualization. Dream vs reality split screen.

sections.title: Around the World: The Same Story
sections.tts_script: This isn't an American problem. The Gini coefficient — the standard measure of inequality — has increased in seventy-one percent of countries since 1990. In China, rapid economic growth lifted hundreds of millions out of poverty. But it also created a new class of ultra-billionaires. The richest one percent of Chinese now hold thirty percent of the country's wealth. In India, the story is sharper still. Mukesh Ambani's net worth grew by more than fifty billion dollars during the pandemic. His monthly income exceeds what the average Indian worker earns in ten lifetimes.
sections.visual_notes: World map Gini coefficient changes. China and India specific data callouts. Timeline 1990-2023.

sections.title: Can It Be Fixed?
sections.tts_script: Oxfam calculated something remarkable. If governments imposed a five percent wealth tax on fortunes above one billion dollars — just five percent — they would raise one point seven trillion dollars every single year. Enough to end extreme poverty twice over. Enough to provide universal healthcare for every low-income country on Earth. Countries like Norway and Denmark have maintained lower inequality through strong unions, free universities, and progressive taxation. Their middle classes are larger. Their social mobility is higher. Their citizens report higher happiness levels. This isn't ideology. It's data. The question isn't whether the wealth gap can be fixed. The question is whether those who benefit from it will ever allow it to be.
sections.visual_notes: Oxfam $1.7T calculation breakdown. Nordic countries comparison data. Happiness index correlation with equality.

sections.title: The Bigger Picture
sections.tts_script: The wealth gap isn't a natural phenomenon. It isn't inevitable. It is the result of specific policies, specific tax codes, specific decisions — made by specific people who benefited from them. Understanding this doesn't make you radical. It makes you informed. If you found this valuable, subscribe. Because the next video in this series is about the other side of this equation — the mechanisms used to hide this wealth. Offshore accounts, shell companies, and the secret financial system that operates above the law.
sections.visual_notes: Closing montage. Subscribe animation. Teaser for next video.
